description:
- D - I - E -

"The Game  of a Thousand Dice"

Deploy your dice, then strategically position rings within the arena to capture enemy dice when they - D - I - E - in a dice fight.

Use captured enemy dice as currency to buy upgrades and earn victory points.

—description from the designer

6 dice each.
Lay out the rope in a ring on the floor.
Take turns throwing dice.
One's are wild and Two's are re-rolls (pick up to throw again later).
Throwing at other players dice to change the value or knock them out of the ring is encouraged!
Winner is the player with the most matching dice.
Only count dice that end up within the ring.
Dice value on matching dice breaks a tie.

—description from the designer

Solo or 2 Player Co-op/Competitive.

Play as 1 or 2 Deck Officers aboard the S.S.C.R.O. (Sol Space Command "Relentless Onslaught"), affectionately called simply “The Crow” by its crew.

Use worker placement to claim ship resources and then allocate those resources to various weapons systems. Spend these resources to fire weapons and launch missiles & fighters at increasingly difficult waves of incoming enemy ships.

Target the correct enemy ships first to earn rewards, trigger combos and prevent certain types of incoming damage.

Enemy ships will damage the Crow’s energy shields and armour plating so some resources will have to be diverted from offensive systems to make repairs.

Upgrade your deck officers capabilities and the Relentless Onslaught itself to survive 6 waves and complete the game. Every wave is randomised so no two games are the same.

Set collection game, where each set you make restricts your collecting options.

Draw varying quantities of coloured tablets from two piles on the table. Each time you make a set of 5, it blocks one of your draw options for that colour. If you have any incomplete sets of 5 at the end of the round they go to the waste tray.

Complete 'prescriptions' and set up cascading combos to be the most efficient pharmacist with the least wasted tablets.

—description from the designer

“The rogue gives chase, as the Twisted King flees into Darkness”

Play cards from your hand to:
Reveal what hides in the darkness.
Improve your deck by collecting upgrades.
Attack and defend against enemies.

Resolve all darkness cards, defeat the Twisted King and escape to the light.

Defeat the darkness enough times and at new difficulty levels to complete the ‘challenge card’.

The Rogue will die, frequently. Complete the ‘death card’ by dying enough times.

—description from the designer

HYPER BEAM
Power Force Ultra
              GT Turbo

Card duelling game between two fighters stuck in a dead lock of opposing energy beams 'tug of war' style.

Draw 5 cards into your hand, top left corner denotes the cards type/ability/value, each card has two options (top and bottom).

After drawing cards, pick whether to use the top or bottom option by rotating cards in your hand so that the chosen option is at the top (these cannot be changed once chosen)

Players take turns playing cards to push the score marker towards their opponent on the score track. If a player pushes the marker to their opponents end of the score track, then that player wins the round.

Best of three rounds, wins.

-description from designer

Order your cannoneers to take aim at enemy forces, use your ammo strategically, and avoid incoming cannonballs to claim victory in Cannon Corps.

Each player uses their favorite set of seven standard role-playing dice (d4, d6, d8, d10, d10%, d12, d20) on a 4-by-3 battlefield grid, obscuring enemies' view of their area. To start, they secretly place four dice on their highest face as platoons on any of their twelve grid spaces.

Players take turns choosing one of their platoons to fire with and one of the twelve grid spaces to aim at. Any enemy die that's hit suffers subtractive damage equal to the value shown on the firing die, and then has the option to move to any adjacent space. If a die fires with a value of 1, everything it hits is reduced to 0. After a die fires it is reduced by half, rounding down.

A die is removed from the battlefield when it is reduced to 0, and its commander replaces it with one of their three initially unused dice. Players are eliminated when their fourth die is reduced to 0, until just one triumphant commander remains!

—description from the designer

Remember the days of the old schoolyard... endless hours spent competing against your buddies in various childhood games; often resolved by Rock-Paper-Scissors or a simple Coin Toss.

Schoolyard Tricks is a trick-taking micro card game for 2 players that will help you to relive some of that old school fun in around 10-20 mins.

From a deck of just 15 cards, each player is dealt 6 cards in 3 suits (Rock-Paper-Scissors).  Players will try to win up to 6 “tricks” each round.  But the twist is that each time both players play Odd numbered cards, the Flip Card swaps to the opposite side - meaning the loser of the trick could win the points.  And with just 15 cards, ties are resolved using Rock-Paper-Scissors - all recorded on a tug-o-war style board.

At the end of the round, the winning player adds their points to their total. Rounds continue until one player reaches 5 points, and are crowned the Schoolyard Champion.

Schoolyard Tricks contains a surprising level of strategy and decision-making for a quick, addictive, and highly portable, 15-card microgame.

-description from designer

Players have to lead a revolutionary organization, eliminate internal conflicts, expect the help of various countries, develop resistance organizations, use both peace and force to consume the energy of the Red Army, and accelerate the end of CCP's rulings.

Or play the role of the Red Army defending the rule of the CCP, defeating all separatists and revolutionaries with an iron fist, guarding social harmony and stability, national unity and national unity, realizing the great rejuvenation of the eastern mainland, and trying to win Taiwan.

Or play the role of the Taiwanese government, using the power of a maritime nation to intervene in the mainland situation, while attacking CCP agents and appeasers in China, using spy networks and covert actions to win this final battle.

From Novosibirsk to Jakarta, from the Pamirs to Sakhalin, 264 cities and towns across the land and sea in the game, unite the 8 countries in the east, mediate the 7 major powers, and liberate the motherland from the iron curtain built by the trade unions!

There are 9 powers for players to play in the game, and each power has different advantages and disadvantages. Different powers can choose different bases, which also means that the powers that can be relied on are different, and different game strategies will be developed accordingly.

"Residuel" is a territory game that uses a uniquely shaped board and hexagonal tiles.

Players play tiles of their own color and compete for the remaining areas where no tiles can be placed. Each area is won by the player with the most tiles adjacent to it, and if there are an equal number of tiles, the area is won by the player who closed it.

This game is characterized by the beauty of the geometric shape of the board and its interesting gameplay, where subtle differences in placement can greatly affect the outcome.

Note that the rules of the game were revised in 2025 for physical publication, so they differ from previous online implementations.

Dansers is a connection game where black tries to connect the north and south edge, and white tries to connect the east and west edge.

Players take turns placing two balls of each colour on the board, which must touch at the end of placement.

—description from the designer

After the King's death two rival fractions compete for the Throne which is situated at the center of a 13-cell Castle.

The board is hexagonal with a Castle at its centre. There's a sufficient amount of checkers. There are two players, Black and White, who each direct an army of men that grows by placement, while the men also move as singles or in line, and may capture conditionally.

Play starts on an empty board and men are entered one by one outside the Castle and must organise themselves to enter it. Inside the Castle the fight goes on as both try to capture the Throne by occupying and holding two cells that are adjacent to it on opposite sides.

—description from the designer

Zurvivors Cooperação is a 2 player game that brings a cooperative mode to the universe of Zurvivors for the first time. With great intelligence and totally silent in order not to attract the zombies, the two players must make combinations of items and actions in a coordinated way to be able to rescue evidence that incriminates the giant corporation behind the incidents, in addition to finding the abandoned motorcycle in order to escape.

boardgame: Eternal: Chronicles of the Throne – Whirly-Twirly
description:
Shuffle the card into the forge deck during set up.
Whirly-Twirly is a Unit card. It has a cost of 4 (with WARP ability upon purchase)and a strengh of 2. When played, draw a card from your deck.

Sabermetrics is a 2-player baseball game, utilizing dice-rolls and mathematics to simulate pitching and hitting, with built in events for stealing bases, picking runners off, and double- and triple-plays. Baseball is a numbers game - and so is Sabermetrics! Battle it out with your opponent over 9 innings in this race for the pennant. Still hungry for more? Why not make it a double-header!

In Sabermetrics, player 1 starts on the mound, pitching three six-sided dice (two white, one green).  Multiply the two white dice together, and then determine if you want to add the green value (for a fastball), or subtract the green value (for a change-up).  That's your pitch number!

Player 2 next rolls the three dice, and uses standard PMDAS (parentheses, multiplication, division, addition, subtraction) to mathematically match the pitch number.  If they can match exactly, that's a single!  Calculate exactly twice the pitch number, and that's a double.  Exactly three times - a triple.  Within 1 - well, that's a ball (good-eye).  Within 2 - that's a strike.  Not even within 2?!  That's an out!  Not even half is a double-play, and not even a third is a triple play (assuming you have baserunners).  Three outs ends the top of the first, and players switch roles (as well as rolls), to start the bottom of the inning.  Roll three-of-a-kind as a pitcher, pick a runner off.  Roll three-of-a-kind as a hitter, steal a base.

After nine innings, the player with the most runs wins -- still tied?  Head to extra innings!  Ever winning by 10 or more at the bottom of an inning?  That's a slaughter rule, take the win, hit the showers early.

There are cubes to track the score, the pitch count, the innings, and the outs.  This game can also be tracked with a standard baseball scorecard sheet as well -- but it's much more fun to use meeple baserunners!

-description from designer

Evacuate is a competitive community deck-building game where players use social deduction to outwit the other players and reach the Evacuation Transport with the most Survivors.

It’s too late! The Nomia plague has spread throughout the space station. The last Evacuation Transport beacon has been activated. There is only one escape transport left and you must lead your people from a shelter to claim it before the others get there first.

You don’t want to rush because you don’t know what is around the next corner, but you also don’t want to be at the back because the slowest always seem to get picked off. The only safe spot is in the middle of the survivors.

Each round players secretly choose a movement card from their hand. Once each player is ready, all cards are revealed and players move their survivor that many spaces, and any actions are activated. Finally, a Nomia card is drawn, possibly killing survivors on the station. The game ends when the evacuation transport is drawn from the deck, and the player with the most remaining survivors wins.

-description from designer

1823 France is an 18xx game set in France.  It has elements of 1817 (loans, mergers, acquisition, liquidation, friendly sales, 1D market), 18MM (randomly distributed revenue locations on the map) and unique things such as plain tiles remaining yellow, but paying to add "spokes".

boardgame: Deep of Mine: Life is Not a Pancake
description:
Humanity is extinct. All the ordinary worries, half-finished things, accumulated stress in search of a new host, went deep down into the wilderness. And who did they found? Of course, cute forest animals! How will these innocent, fluffy little creatures deal with mental illnesses that came from nowhere and had previously been completely unknown to them?

Be a part of their everyday lives as the rest of their lives start right now, but it could easily end in psychiatry. It's all up to you! (Evil laugh)

How to play? It's easy!

The table before the player is the player's Mind. Here you have to play and collect your Moment cards, like Video Gaming, Unkept Promises, Mowing the Lawn. You can play negative cards (like Phobia, Illness - eg. Coulrophobia, Insomnia) to other player's Moments, or positive cards (Recovery - eg. Aversion Therapy, Faith in the Future) to your Moments. These cards will affect your Mentality value plus or minus. There are immediate cards like Impact cards (eg. The Swamp of Sorrow, Salmon of Doubt), which will alter the gameplay a bit by the card effect, and Fracture cards (eg. Bipolar Disorder, Panic Disorder), which will drive a punch into your Mentality.

This is a turn based game. One turn has 3 phases. Phase 1 is your card's "beginning of your turn" phase. Cards have effects, so some of the cards will apply their effect here. Phase 2 is Defragmentation Phase. The player has got 2 Fragments, and a Fragment can be redeemed into Actions, like DRAW a card or PLAY a card from the hand. The last Phase is the end Phase, the player has to keep the hand limit.

You can STEAL, ERASE, LOCK, etc. a card(s) from your so called friends' Mind. You may decide to be evil - it never hurts. When you erase a Moment, it goes into that player's own Subconscious discard pile.

The player who first collects 7 Moments in his/her Mind and his/her Mentality reaches or exceeds 15 (in a positive direction, of course) wins the game. It's that simple and great.

-description from publisher

Backyard cricket is a roll and write for 1 to 10 players, that takes the high scoring of runs, plenty of wickets and always some not outs, of playing cricket in the backyard. From 8 runs for on the roof and off again to 5 runs for hitting the clothes line. Roll the ball dice and the extras dice to make runs fast while watching out for those pesky stumps!

Players roll the dice then while matching numbers with their score sheet trying to skip over the wickets, play continues until some one either gets 10 wickets or finishes their 4th innings. Count up the total runs scored, the player with the most, Wins.

An escape room experience from the Curious Correspondence Club.  The first season is broken into 12 chapters, of which, The Custodian's Keys are first.

You will work through a series of puzzles to solve each chapter at your own pace with a digital hint system available if needed.

Welcome to the M.B. Franklin Museum of Natural History, a place of knowledge, learning, and curiosity.

When a serendipitous discovery grants you an audience with the Museum’s Custodian, a peculiar occurrence launches the hunt for a secret hidden within this storied institution.

Will you be able to unlock the secrets?

---

Audience
Recommended for 12+; younger players may require guidance from an adult. Designed to be played either solo or collaboratively, and takes an average of 60-90 minutes to solve, though players can work through them at their own pace.

Difficulty
Each Chapter of The CCC is supported by a dedicated digital hint library, allowing you to choose how many additional clues you receive. From subtle nudges all the way to complete solutions for each puzzle, it's up to you to choose your level of difficulty.

-description from publisher's website

It’s the 1970s. Hit the road with your band to spread peace, love and unity through the power of music. In this feel-good game, players compete to see who can spread the most global unity.

The game’s currency is love and it is gained by performing at concerts. During a turn, the active player moves to spaces on the board to set up concerts, collect fans and place them on their concerts. The more fans at your concert, the more love you gain when you perform there. Players draft musicians with different abilities depending on which instrument they play. Musician's abilities must be used sparingly, as they fatigue after they perform. Players can use ganja to revive their band members.

Bob Marley is represented in the game and he gives options when you interact with him. Players may also strategically place Marley at one of their own concerts to prompt other players to attend. Two types of cards are gained that either give bonuses that may shift strategy, or give goals to achieve extra unity points at the end of the game.

The end goal is to perform at the One Love concert where you cash in your love for unity. The game ends when all the unity tokens are collected. Then all players tally unity tokens and additional unity point bonuses to see who is the victor. But really, everybody wins in a world with a little more unity!

-description from publisher

A fun take on the classic game of golf cards. Golf Nut crazy pokes fun at the cast of characters at your local golf course. You don't need to love golf to play, just a sense of humor and a bit of a competitive streak!

Like golf, the payer with the lowest score wins! With nine holes to play, you'll need your wits about you as you battle your opponents. Just watch out for the wild characters that can really throw a wrench in your score.

How to play: 

     You get one character card and four playing cards for each of the 9 "holes" aka rounds
    - Draw a card - keep it or swap it 
     Reveal one of your four cards
     The hole ends when all players reveal their four cards
     The lowest score after nine holes wins!


—description from the designer

Family Fun!
No more time spent thinking up good charades - the fun starts as soon as the box is opened. With both easy and challenging charades, and with charades for different tastes and interests, Family Charades is THE perfect game for your next family game night.

Streets of Steel is a 1-4 player co-operative sidescroller, where players brawl their way through baddies in a 1980's-esque, pixelated, post-apocalyptic, degenerate cityscape. Fight filthy Punks, hot-headed Firebugs, and elusive Killgaroos (yup, ninja kangaroos) in order to take down the big boss, Super Mutie.

This is a fast paced and funny game with interesting new boardgame mechanics and lots of reverence for 16-bit, arcade fighting.

The game is for 1-4 players, and plays in under an hour. Players will join together to beat down waves of baddies by rolling lots of dice, risking lots of resources, busting crates, using items and defeating the big boss. After each full round of Hero and Baddie activations, the game board sidescrolls… everything on the first tile dies as it is removed, and then a new tile pops in and spawns another wave of baddies. Players choose their own difficulty by starting the game with a limited number of quarters. Make it to the Boss, or die trying, as you try to clean up the STREETS OF STEEL!

—description from the designer

Bargo is a Go-like territory game for two players: Black and White. It is played on the spaces (cells) of an initially empty hexagonal grid of hexagons (board). Each player must have access to a sufficient number of hexagonal pieces of their own color.

Each piece has one of its six edge segments covered with a wall. The outer edges of the board are also covered with walls.

				
				
					DefinitionsTwo adjacent cells, and any pieces on them, are linked if their shared edge segment does not contain a wall.

A group is a piece along with all other like-colored pieces one could reach from it through a series of steps between linked pieces of its color.

A liberty of a group is an empty cell linked to at least one piece in that group.

Similarly, a territory is an empty cell along with all other empty cells one could reach from it through a series of steps between linked empty cells. You own a territory if all pieces linked to cells in that territory are of your color.

				
				
					PlayBlack plays first, then turns alternate. On your turn, you must pass or place a piece of your color on an empty cell without causing any edge segment on the board to contain more than one wall, not even momentarily during the move.

After a placement, remove all enemy pieces in groups without liberties. After all removals, the following conditions must be met:


     Your newly placed piece must be part of a group with at least one liberty.
     The cell on the other side of the wall of your newly placed piece must not contain an enemy piece.
     The current board position, ignoring walls, must be different from the board positions at the end of all your previous turns.


Otherwise, your placement is illegal.

The game ends when both players pass in succession. The player with the higher score in the final position wins. A player's score is the number of pieces of their color on the board, plus the number of cells in their territories, plus a komi in the case of White. The button is used to avoid ties.

				
				
					Komi and buttonThe komi is the whole number of points which is added to White's score at the end of the game as a compensation for playing second. Before the game starts, the first player chooses the value of komi, and then the second player chooses sides. Alternatively, experienced players may agree on a standard value for all games.

The button is a special token which is placed next to the board at the start of the game. On your turn, if neither player has taken the button yet, you may not pass, but you may take the button instead of making a board play. At the end of the game, a half point is added to the score of the player who has taken the button. When checking for repetitions, two identical board positions are still considered different if the button had already been taken in one of them but not in the other.

				
				
					NotesLike its companion game Keil, Bargo aims to preserve crosscuts and ko from Go by reducing the natural connectivity of the hexagonal grid.

-description from designer

Nume is a territory game for two players: Black and White. It is played on the spaces (cells) of an initially empty hexagonal grid of hexagons with sides alternating in length between n and n + 2 cells. Each player must have access to a sufficient number of stones of their own color and a pen to write on them.

				
				
					DefinitionsA group is a stone along with all other like-colored stones one could reach from it through a series of steps between adjacent stones of its color.

The size of a group is the number of stones in it.

The price of a group is the highest number obtainable by selecting, from that group, a stone that has a number written on it and computing the absolute value of the difference between that number and the size of that group.

				
				
					PlayBlack plays first, then turns alternate. On your turn, place a stone of your color on an empty cell. If this stone creates a new group, write a whole number on it.

The game ends when the board is full. The player with the lower score in the final position wins. Your score is the sum of the prices of all your groups on the board, plus a komi when applicable.

				
				
					KomiThe komi is the number of points which is added to the score of one of the players to offset turn order advantage. To avoid ties, the value of komi must be of the form n + 0.5, where n is a whole number.

Before the game starts, the first player chooses the value of komi and which side will receive it, and then the second player chooses sides. Alternatively, experienced players may agree on a standard value and recipient side for all games. A half-point komi for Black is suggested as a default value.

				
				
					VariantsSquare: Played on the intersections of a square grid. In the definition of group, "adjacent" means only "orthogonally adjacent". To avoid mirror play, either use an odd-sized board or always give a komi to Black.

—description from the designer

Deluxe Edition includes:


     Chocolate Factory: Master Chocolatier Mini-Expansion inside the box.
     Thick Factory Part tiles instead of mini-cards.
     Large screen-printed wooden pieces for the Start Player and Day markers.
     Chunky plastic coal pieces.


Set in the early 20th century of entrepreneurs and chocolatiers experimenting with new ways to create and market chocolates, YOU are assigned the task of chief chocolate maker! Chocolate Factory is a euro game of literal factory building and moving (using a pushable conveyer belt component) chocolates you must complete the largest number of chocolates required by retailers for the best value. The game also features wooden chocolate pieces and a movable conveyer belt making the physicality blended with euro mechanics a unique experience.

Gameplay overview:
Players are each given a starting player board which will have two Factory parts which apply to a physical conveyer belt. The conveyer belt part of the player board contains long slot with an empty region in which to push square tiles along. The tiles will house the chocolate pieces players will manipulate throughout the game.

During the game players will initially draft new factory parts (to add to their player board) and specialists (one time use cards with special powers).

Then each player will “push” three times cocoa into their machines. During each “push” players will be able to use fuel and their factory parts to manipulate their Chocolate pieces. Careful consideration will be required for the spatial element of the game as Chocolate pieces can only be manipulated during a push by the Factory part that is adjacent to their tile.

Players score points by creating chocolates for public and personal objectives. The player with the most points at the end wins.

description:
BUILD YOUR GUILD. SLAY MONSTERS. TAKE THE THRONE.

WarRift is a competitive and cooperative tabletop deck-building game with a focus on player interaction. Your guild competes and slays the invading monsters to save the Kingdom of Nayvar and take the throne. WarRift brings fresh mechanics to the genre, like each player having their own signature buy-pool (guild), instead of all players getting cards from the same spot.

If you don't pay the extra cost on a card, it goes into the Rift. Cards in the Rift have a chance of an opponent using it for one turn, making for some powerful plays. There's also bartering, intense player-elimination, the ability for players to hold cards in their hand and time their combos, monster bosses, casual and solo formats, and a lot more. With all its elements at play, WarRift creates a completely unique deck-building experience fit for a KING!

Rules and rulings and more can be found at WarRift.com

boardgame: Departo 44: Devenez le meilleur guide de Loire-Atlantique
description:
Departo 44: Devenez le meilleur guide de Loire-Atlantique lets you become the best guide in the "Loire-Atlantique" department through questions about history, geography, culture...

Gameplay:

     Take a coach trip through the Loire-Atlantique department and answer questions,

The atmosphere is not good in the house: one of your roommates has a problem. For everything to be in order we will have to cooperate to listen to him and find solutions with him. And this, before Spleen, the incarnation of dark ideas, enters, otherwise it is the guaranteed mess!

boardgame: The Battle of Bunker Hill: June 17th 1775
description:
This is a battle that has fascinated players over the years and this game is a real challenge for them. It shows the bravery and doggedness of the British against the abilities of the fledgling Americans Rebels. In their leaders, Howe must attempt to overwhelm the enemy, while Prescott had to organise a defence against them out of nothing. To achieve this, we have written a set of rules to showpiece the distinctive way this battle was fought.
 Each side has a unique set of circumstances and using miniature forces on hexes, manoeuvreing, dice rolls, fire and melee the players must face the problems the leaders of the actual battle had to overcome. In this particular case, the Regular British Line must overpower the semi trained Revolutionary Militia of 1775 in their defensive terrain.
 The core of the game is aimed at the players, this means that all players should be involved throughout the game. Not having to wait until one player has finished all their moves and firing. The action should be continuous for all players. Just because you have the best soldiers you should not automatically win. The game should be enjoyable and everyone should have a chance to win.

A set of rules that showpieces the distinctive way this battle was fought and highlights the bravery and doggedness of the British against the emerging abilities of the fledgling American Rebels. Lead the American Militia to defend the works on Breeds Hill or send your doughty British Regulars into attack again and again! The fate of a nation is your hands, its your choice.

Blarney Stones is a betting game.  The board has 10 spaces with a 3 letter word on each.  The words all begin with P, end with either T or N, and have vowels as middle letters.  3 D6 letter dice are thrown to make one of the words on the board.  One die has all Ps, but includes a different number on each face.  The second has either an N or a T.  The third has the five vowels and a blank.  Before the dice are thrown, players bet on which word will come up.  They may bet multiple chips on one word or more.  (The chips are all one color, so players need to note how many they played on each word.) The dice thrower is the banker who does not bet.  The banker pays the winner(s) the chips they bet multiplied by the number showing on the P die, but collects the chips from the non-winning spaces.  Should a blank be thrown on the vowel die, the banker pays all players, but only chip for chip.  When this occurs, the banker position rotates to the player on the left.

Interestingly, no win condition is stated, though one, such as last man standing, can easily be made up.

In Frontier Valley you will hire workers, learn about their abilities and employ them as farmers to produce goods on your land, as craftsmen to make the building material you buy with your produce and as townsfolk to make use of your new facilities. You will need to make sure to make good use of the land so you are the player to better satisfy the market's demands. But beware: one misjugded applicant, one thoughtless land lease or one greedy building project could be all that is needed for the luck to turn towards your opponent!

Frontier Valley is a two-player 18 card game of deduction, market manipulation, building structures and powerful one-time actions. Each round you choose one worker card from your hand to use. This might sound easy, but as in Hanabi, you do not see your own cards, only the ones of your opponent, so make sure you make the right pick even when you do not have perfect information! You then use this worker as farmer to plant new crops and thus changing the price of produce on the market, as builder to erect new structures (if you can sell valuable goods) that bring victory points or as townsfolk to use powerful one-time actions on your already standing buildings. Then you will have to give your opponent some hints of her choosing toward the cards in her hand. Will you manage to outsmart your opponent by manipulating the supply of farming goods such that you always have the most valuable produce? Will you be able to hire a sought after seasoned architect to build the most impressive town buildings? Whoever has the buildings with the most victory points in total will be the winner of Frontier Valley.

The game features a beginner mode for learning the interactions of hints, the type of workers there are and the market dynamics. Once you played this at least once, feel free to add the one-time actions of townsfolk to the game for added complexity and challenge.

-description from designer

Crash Factor is a truly strategic pure balancing game played with calibrated weights.
Players take turns in a race to be rid of their "fleet" of varied weights by playing them onto a balancing gameboard into risk-reward zones.
Causing a crash or being unable to make a play during the game adds a dangerous penalty piece to the fleet.
A concentric "Crash Factor" grid allows players to anticipate the crash risk of any weight placed anywhere.
Playing risky moves away from the center of balance can win advantages or eliminate penalties.

GAMEPLAY BASICS:
A win is triggered once a player has deployed all their spaceships and space junk.

- Flip over a "mission" card to reveal a value.
- You must match that value with the weight value of any quantity of spaceships deployed anywhere onto the solar system.
- The farther from the center a ship is deployed, the greater the risk and greater the reward.
- You may "dock" (stack) them onto any other spaceship to have their values added together towards making the required match.
- Get a space junk penalty piece if unable to make a match or by causing a crash.
- After a crash, the empty solar system gets re-balanced to continue the game.
- Get rid of your space junk or risk being eliminated by getting a 2nd one.
- Strategically manage the deployment of your light and heavy ships as your fleet diminishes while the game advances.
- Look for or create opportunities to deploy into high risk zones to gain significant advantages or eliminate a penalty.
- Tactically play special utility devices anytime during a mission.
- Ruthlessly capture an opponent's resources by docking onto their spaceship.
- You can choose one of three play options instead of choosing a mission:
1) Jettison a piece of space junk.
2) Take back a needed spaceship.
3) Swap an un-deployed heavy ship for its equivalent weight from your lighter ships that have been deployed.

Play at your own risk!

Counting Campers is the fun counting game for your little camper. Players move their pegs forward or backward along the hiking trail by drawing a ‘plus’ or ‘minus’ card. Follow the winding path through the muddy swamp, across the fishing pond, over the old bridge, and more, to get back to the campsite. The first ‘camper’ back to camp wins the game!

In more detail, each player chooses a Camper and places it in the starting space on the game board in its matching color trail. Shuffle the cards and place them to the side of the board, facedown. Let the camping trip begin!

On your turn, draw a card from the playing deck. If a plus card (+) is drawn, jump your Camper forward the number shown. If a minus (-) card is drawn, move backwards the number shown. It's a great way for young players to practice counting skills and learn the concept of plus (+) and minus (-) while having fun!

Watch for special Camper cards while you play! If a card is drawn with a happy Camper with a sun/rainbow, draw two more cards and move. If a card is drawn with an unhappy Camper under a rain cloud, you lose a turn.

The first Camper to reach the campsite wins! For additional challenge for older players, the exact number card must be drawn in order to move the remaining spaces to the finish.

On 1 May 3052, over twenty Galaxies from seven Clans clashed with twelve Armies of Comstar's Com Guards. For twenty-one days, one of the largest campaigns in BattleMech warfare unfolded to decide the fate of the Inner Sphere.

The Battle of Tukayyid brings players back to this gargantuan conflict, allowing them to relive one of BattleTech's most pivotal moments. It builds off of the Chaos Campaign: Succession Wars framework to unleash a massive experience players will enjoy across dozens of games. Each of the seven campaigns within includes the following: a short fiction entry and write-up of the battle, including a map of the regions; the specific Combatants involved, along with insignia; and details to allow players to play out each campaign on their tabletop. Finally, a technical readout section showcases 'Mech variants that first appeared during this conflict.

This Chaos Campaign puts you as the hero -- Aidan Pryde, Anastatius Focht, and others -- of the battle. Do you have what it takes to lead the Smoke Jaguars to victory, or will you repeat the failure of the Diamond Sharks? Or can you find a way to defeat the indomitable Clan Wolf? You will decide.

For use with: Clan Invasion box set, MapPack: Battle of Tukayyid, and compatible with Total Warfare and Alpha Strike.

You have languished for what feels like an eternity in the prison of an unjust king, but today your sentence has been adjusted to fit his fancy. He has demanded your participation in a grueling set of trials against the foulest creatures in the kingdom. The deal is simple: Survive the test and the king will grant your freedom. In order to maximize the king's enjoyment, you will not be alone and you will be allowed time to prepare.

King's Eye is a cooperative, drafting battle simulator for 2-4 players. Choose a character, draft a deck full of upgrades and equipment, and battle through 7 waves of mobs.

King's Eye is played in 2 major phases. In phase 1, players draft upgrade decks in a traditional pass drafting format using 3 "packs" of 9 cards per player from the 120 included upgrade cards. These cards improve character stats and add new abilities/actions that players will be able to utilize in phase 2. At the end of the draft, players have a 27 card deck full of exciting enhancements with which to power-up their character.

Phase 2 is a cooperative battle through 7 waves of mobs. Each round a number of mobs equal to the number of players joins the battle, players take their turns, and then any remaining mobs take their turns. The 7 waves are split into 3 waves each of basic and intermediate mobs, followed by a boss mob wave at the end. Throughout the wave battle, players are becoming stronger and stronger as they seek to stay alive and dispatch the enemies. Once the boss mobs arrive, players have 3 rounds to clear all enemies. Any player that is still alive after all of the enemies have been defeated shares the victory, earning their freedom and the joy that comes from seeing the shock in the King's Eye.

On your turn, you draw a card from your upgrade deck and choose a card in your hand to play. Then you can choose 2 actions to perform from you list of available actions.  Everyone has certain action options: draw a card, play a card, perform a basic attack. Each character, and many upgrade cards offer additional action options for players to utilize.

NewSpeak is a tense game of dystopian subversion in which you take the role of either the 'dissidents' or the 'moderators' in a mind-bending battle of wits to determine the reality around you.

Will you challenge the status quo and shatter your fragile reality or seek to protect the safety of what you know?

Dissidents utilise matching underground codes to coordinate meetings at one of the disturbingly illustrated locations on your map. They then each secretly use their location dials to declare their intended meeting point.

The Mods, in a short amount of time, then have to determine where they expect the Dissidents to meet by inferring their intent from a book of every possible code set the subversives could use, and agree upon a location to direct a state raiding party.

Once they've agreed on a location, the Dissidents each reveal their location dials. Depending on whether the majority of Dissidents have successfully coordinated at a single location, or the Mods have caught them in an attempt at subversion will determine whether that location becomes 'hacked' or 'moderated'.

As the game progresses, moderated locations produce potential accomplices for the Dissidents to recruit, as undesirables are driven from now sanitized districts. However, as the Dissidents progress their mission to 'hack' as many locations as possible, the Moderators strike back with increasing numbers of surveillance outposts, installed to limit the Dissident's ability to move around the city undetected.

NewSpeak: Deluxe Edition includes the NewSpeak base game and two expansions - Rated and Sabotage - all packaged inside one box.

Candidate is a two-player loosely-themed abstract game played on a 5 x 5 grid.  Each player has a set of the same 5 pieces in their own color.  There are 3 types of pieces; 1 candidate, 2 party, 2 media.  These pieces are square, with numbers along each edge.  The game begins with each player's set against that player's side of the board with the orientation and placement of each piece as described in the instructions.  In the course of the game, the players try to move all their pieces to the opposite side of the board with the first one doing so winning the game.  In a turn, one piece is moved one space or rotated 90° during each move.  When a piece from one player is moved adjacent to one of the other, an Overpowering Move occurs, where the piece having the higher number on the touching side allowing that player to push the opponent's piece one space in any direction.

Place a deck of cards with questions taken from four categories (people and history, science and the world, miscellaneous, sports and entertainment) and a hexagonal score mat divided into six sections (including START) at the center. Each player/team choose a triangular mover and place it on the START space.

Oldest player draws a card from the deck and reads aloud the question on the front of the card to everybody, stating the three possible answers.

First person to clap their hands together gets to answer the question. The reader of the card decides all ties. If they are correct, that player or team moves their triangle ahead one space. The card is placed in a discard pile. If they are incorrect, the reader will indicate they are incorrect and any other player or team gets a chance to answer by immediately clapping their hands first. If nobody gets it, the card goes into the discard pile.

The player who answered the question correctly then draws and reads the next question. Play continues until a player or team has answered five questions correctly.

Includes score mat + 90 cards + 4 triangle movers. Play one-on-one or split players into up to four teams.

Für die Katz is the cooperative children’s game, in which animal voices make a path audible that cannot be seen.
Together the players bring cats from the forest back into their baskets. But in the forest it is dark. Blindfolded, one player always guides his walking stick through the impassable terrain. The other players guide it to where the cats have lost their way. Good listening is required: From which direction is the dog barking? Is a goat bleating? And where does the eagle owl point the way? All cats can be rescued and falling branches can be avoided by attentive cooperation.

—description from the publisher

Mosaic Quo is a Mosaic set exclusively for four players. It's played the same as the base game, but it has four sets of distinctly colored pieces on the same 7x7 board.


     Starting with the first player, place your pieces one by one in the empty hole of the game board.
     If four pieces are lined up in the smallest square shape adjacent to each other, pieces can be placed on them.
     If four frames are arranged in a square, and three or four of the four are occupied by the same color, the frame of that color is automatically placed on it (apart from the turn).
     The piece fixed from the beginning to the center of the game board becomes a neutral piece which is not that of either player. When a square containing neutral frames is created, frames are automatically placed on top of the square only if the remaining three frames have the same color.
     Automatically placed pieces will create new squares, and if 3 or 4 of the squares are occupied with the same color, pieces will be placed automatically as well. In this way, pieces are stacked in a chain reaction. The pieces to be stacked may not only be the pieces of the active player, but also the pieces of the opposing player.
     In this way, the person who put the piece in turn alternately and who finished all his pieces first wins.


—description from publisher

UNAUTHORIZED PRODUCTION

Mosaic Mini has a 5x5 board and fewer pieces, but it is otherwise the same as the larger 7x7 edition Mosaic.

The game is functionally identical to Margalith Akavya's Upper Hand.
